title: Signing of the Treaty of Versailles
summary: On 28 June 1919, five years to the day after the Sarajevo assassination that had triggered the First World War, the German plenipotentiaries Hermann Muller and Johannes Bell signed a punitive peace treaty in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles. The Treaty of Versailles stripped Germany of colonies and territory, restricted its armed forces, blamed it for the war under Article 231, and imposed reparations that would burden its economy through the 1920s. It also created the League of Nations. Almost every strand of interwar politics ran, for good or ill, through its clauses.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: The Council of Four at the Paris peace conference: Lloyd George, Orlando, Clemenceau, and Wilson.
image.credit: Edward N. Jackson, US Army Signal Corps, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The armistice signed in a railway carriage at Compiegne on 11 November 1918 halted the guns but left the terms of peace to be worked out at a conference in Paris that opened on 18 January 1919, the anniversary of the proclamation of the German Empire in the same Hall of Mirrors half a century before. Around thirty-two Allied and Associated states sent delegations, but the real decisions were taken by the Council of Four: US president Woodrow Wilson, British prime minister David Lloyd George, French premier Georges Clemenceau, and Italian premier Vittorio Emanuele Orlando. Germany, Austria, Hungary, Bulgaria, and Turkey were excluded from the negotiations; Soviet Russia was neither invited nor recognised.

The Allies came to Paris with sharply different aims. Wilson had set out in January 1918 his Fourteen Points, calling for open diplomacy, freedom of the seas, national self-determination, the reduction of armaments, and a general association of nations. Clemenceau, whose country had lost a million and a half war dead and had had its northern departments turned into a lunar landscape by four years of Western Front fighting, wanted permanent guarantees of French security: a demilitarised Rhineland, a large reparations bill, and if possible the detachment of the Left Bank from Germany. Lloyd George, victorious in a December 1918 election fought on the slogan of making Germany pay, was under domestic pressure for a hard peace but privately worried that too harsh a settlement would breed a war of revenge. Orlando pursued Italian claims in the Adriatic promised at the secret Treaty of London of 1915.

The Germans had asked for an armistice in the expectation that any peace would be built on Wilson's Fourteen Points and that they would be treated as partners in a new order. Instead they were kept at bay in a Versailles hotel while the terms were drafted without them. By early May 1919 the draft treaty, some 440 articles long, was ready. It was presented to the German delegation on 7 May 1919. The German foreign minister Ulrich Graf von Brockdorff-Rantzau, refusing to stand, denounced the terms and returned to Berlin. For weeks the new Weimar Republic government agonised. A cabinet resigned; Field Marshal Paul von Hindenburg admitted that the German army could not resume a war of resistance. On 22 June the National Assembly meeting at Weimar authorised acceptance under protest, and a new government headed by the Social Democrat Gustav Bauer was formed to sign.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: William Orpen's official painting of the signing in the Hall of Mirrors, 28 June 1919.
image.credit: William Orpen, Imperial War Museums, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
On the afternoon of 28 June 1919 the Allied delegations, delegates from many smaller states, journalists, and photographers filled the Hall of Mirrors, the seventy-three-metre-long gallery whose ceiling had been painted for Louis XIV. Two chairs at a small table faced the semicircle of Allied signatories. At three o'clock Clemenceau, presiding as premier of the host power, ordered the German representatives brought in. Hermann Muller, the Social Democrat foreign minister, and Johannes Bell, the Centre Party minister of transport, walked to the table and, with a nib pen, signed the treaty first. President Wilson, Lloyd George, Clemenceau, and Orlando then signed for their governments, followed by the other Allied delegates, in a ceremony lasting less than an hour. Outside, guns of the Versailles garrison fired a salute; fountains that had been silent since 1914 were turned on.

The treaty ran to fifteen parts. Part I established the Covenant of the League of Nations. Territorial clauses (parts II and III) returned Alsace and Lorraine to France, awarded Eupen-Malmedy to Belgium, most of Posen and West Prussia to a resurrected Poland, and northern Schleswig to Denmark after a plebiscite; Danzig became a free city under League authority, the Saar coal basin was placed under League administration for fifteen years, and the Rhineland was demilitarised. Germany renounced all its overseas colonies, which were distributed as League mandates chiefly to Britain, France, Japan, and the dominions. The military clauses (part V) limited the German army to 100,000 men without conscription, banned tanks, military aircraft, submarines, and heavy artillery, and reduced the navy to a coast-defence force. Part VIII on reparations included the notorious Article 231, the 'war guilt clause', which required Germany and its allies to accept responsibility for causing all the loss and damage suffered by the Allies. A specific reparations sum was left to a commission, which fixed it in 1921 at 132 billion gold marks.

Separate treaties were negotiated with each of the other defeated powers over the following months and years: Saint-Germain-en-Laye with Austria in September 1919, Neuilly with Bulgaria in November 1919, Trianon with Hungary in June 1920, and Sevres with the Ottoman Empire in August 1920, later replaced by Lausanne in 1923 after Turkish resistance under Mustafa Kemal. Together they redrew the map of Europe and the Middle East.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The treaty was ratified by the German National Assembly and came into force on 10 January 1920 with the deposit of instruments in Paris. It was not ratified by the United States: the Senate, led by Republican Henry Cabot Lodge, refused to accept the League Covenant with its Article 10 collective security guarantee, and Wilson's insistence on the treaty as a whole doomed both. Washington concluded a separate peace with Germany in 1921 and never joined the League that Wilson had done more than any other statesman to design.

In Germany the treaty was received as a Diktat, an imposed peace, and became a poisoned inheritance of the Weimar Republic. Reparations, restructured twice under the Dawes Plan (1924) and the Young Plan (1929), coincided with the hyperinflation of 1923, when the French and Belgians occupied the Ruhr to enforce coal deliveries, and with the world Depression. The 'war guilt' clause, whose legal drafters had intended it narrowly as a basis for compensation, was widely read as a moral verdict and rejected by German public opinion across the political spectrum. Nazi propaganda would make its repudiation a central plank of Adolf Hitler's programme, and by 1935-36 he had unilaterally torn up its military and territorial clauses.

East of the Rhine the settlement produced or confirmed a belt of successor states, Poland, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia, Austria, Hungary, that were smaller, ethnically mixed, and often at odds with one another. In the Middle East, mandates over former Ottoman provinces went to Britain (Palestine, Transjordan, Iraq) and France (Syria, Lebanon), setting the political frame for later crises. In East Asia the transfer of German rights in Shandong to Japan, rather than back to China, ignited the May Fourth Movement in Beijing in 1919 and radicalised a generation of Chinese intellectuals. The League itself functioned through the 1920s and had genuine successes on refugees, health, and labour standards, but its failure to punish Japanese aggression in Manchuria (1931), Italian aggression in Ethiopia (1935), or German remilitarisation left it a dead letter by 1939.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Few peace treaties in modern history have been so intensively studied or so severely judged. The verdict of the British economist John Maynard Keynes, who resigned in protest from the British delegation and published The Economic Consequences of the Peace in December 1919, that Versailles was a Carthaginian peace which would beggar Germany and destabilise Europe, became the dominant liberal criticism for a generation. A rival tradition, articulated between the wars and after by historians such as Etienne Mantoux, argued that reparations were bearable, that Germany paid only a fraction of what was demanded, and that it was Nazi choices, not treaty clauses, that produced the next war.

More recent scholarship tends to emphasise the treaty's contradictions rather than its cruelty. It applied Wilson's principle of self-determination selectively, denying it to Germans in Austria and the Sudetenland while granting it to Czechs and Poles. It preserved great chunks of the old dynastic order in the Middle East and in Africa under new mandate labels. It punished Germany hard enough to breed resentment but not hard enough to disable it as a great power. The reparations regime tied European recovery to American lending and helped transmit the Wall Street crash across the Atlantic in 1929-31.

What is not in doubt is the treaty's role as a template. Its architects invented the League of Nations, the ancestor of the United Nations; the modern practice of war crimes trials, foreshadowed in the still-born plan to try the ex-Kaiser; the mandate system that morphed into UN trusteeship; and the linkage between minority rights, ethnic borders, and international guarantees. In museums from Versailles to Berlin, the ornate treaty book with its many signatures still sits behind glass as one of the founding documents of the modern world, contested and canonical at once.

worldContext.text: The Versailles decision to hand German rights in Shandong to Japan, rather than restoring them to China, provoked mass student protests in Beijing on 4 May 1919. The May Fourth Movement transformed Chinese intellectual life, energising vernacular literature, feminist reform, and, within two years, the founding of the Chinese Communist Party in Shanghai. Japan, seated at the top table of the Paris conference for the first time as a great power, was frustrated that its proposed racial-equality clause for the League Covenant had been quietly dropped.

India in the spring of 1919 was in political ferment. On 13 April Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer had ordered his troops to fire on an unarmed crowd in an enclosed garden at Amritsar, killing several hundred; the Rowlatt Acts extending wartime security laws had provoked Gandhi's first all-India satyagraha. In Afghanistan the Third Anglo-Afghan War of May-August 1919 ended with the Treaty of Rawalpindi and Afghan control of foreign relations. In Central Asia the Bolsheviks and Basmachi insurgents fought in Bukhara and Khiva.

The Ottoman Empire was being partitioned outside the Paris hall. Britain and France, using the wartime Sykes-Picot understanding as a template, established mandates over Iraq, Palestine, Transjordan, Syria, and Lebanon. In Anatolia the landing of Greek troops at Smyrna on 15 May 1919 galvanised a Turkish national resistance under Mustafa Kemal that would ultimately overturn the Sevres settlement and produce the Republic of Turkey in 1923. In Egypt a wide anti-British revolution had broken out in March 1919 under Saad Zaghloul's Wafd Party.

The redistribution of Germany's African colonies as League mandates dominated the continent's international politics. Tanganyika went to Britain, Rwanda-Urundi to Belgium, Cameroon and Togoland were split between Britain and France, and South-West Africa was placed under South African rule. In Ethiopia the young Ras Tafari Makonnen, the future Emperor Haile Selassie, was consolidating power as regent. Pan-African organising received an early boost with the Pan-African Congress convened in Paris in February 1919 by W.E.B. Du Bois on the margins of the peace conference.

In the United States the Senate was already fighting over the treaty as it was being signed; over the summer and autumn of 1919 Wilson embarked on a whistle-stop speaking tour to rally support, collapsing with a serious stroke in October. The Red Summer of 1919 brought race riots to Chicago, Washington, and dozens of other American cities. Mexico continued its revolutionary aftershocks; Emiliano Zapata had been assassinated in April 1919. In Argentina the Semana Tragica strikes of January exposed the limits of Radical reformism.

Australia, New Zealand, and South Africa signed the Versailles Treaty as autonomous members of the British Empire, an early recognition of dominion status in international law. All three received former German colonies as class C mandates: Australia took German New Guinea and Nauru (with Britain and New Zealand), New Zealand took Western Samoa, and South Africa took South-West Africa. In the Pacific the first attempts to fly the Atlantic and to reach Australia from Britain by aeroplane were being made; Ross and Keith Smith would land at Darwin in December 1919.

figures.name: Woodrow Wilson
figures.role: President of the United States, principal author of the League of Nations Covenant
figures.credit: Harris & Ewing, US Library of Congress, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Georges Clemenceau
figures.role: Premier of France and chair of the peace conference, pressing for strong French security guarantees

figures.name: David Lloyd George
figures.role: British prime minister, balancing domestic demands for a hard peace against strategic caution

figures.name: Vittorio Emanuele Orlando
figures.role: Italian premier, walked out for a time over unmet Adriatic claims

figures.name: Hermann Muller
figures.role: German foreign minister, one of the two signatories for Germany

figures.name: Johannes Bell
figures.role: German transport minister and co-signatory for Germany

images.caption: William Orpen's official painting of the signing in the Hall of Mirrors, 28 June 1919, with the German delegates seated at the small table facing the Allied leaders.
images.credit: William Orpen, Imperial War Museums collection, via Wikimedia Commons
